innovation_hook: Solve the inverse problem: pick target gifts first, then back out the cheapest schedule supporting them as best responses -- forward tweaking of match rates cannot escape the crowd-out fixed point.
why_it_generalizes: Subsidies that must pay only for marginal behavior (matching grants, performance bonuses, carbon credits) all share the counterfactual-shifting trap; inverse mechanism design is the reusable move.
seed_example: Input: 30k donors with printed warm-glow utility parameters; output: a piecewise-linear match schedule whose total match payout at the induced equilibrium must be <= B (checked). verify.py computes each donor's closed-form optimal gift under the schedule and scores total donations. Trap: a flat 1:1 match burns most of B on gifts the planted mass of high-warm-glow donors would have made anyway; the ceiling needs convex-kinked schedules paying only above counterfactual levels -- but kink placement itself moves the best responses that define those counterfactuals.

innovation_hook: Two structurally different imperfections (rational gain, hysteretic deadband) are confounded in training data and can only be separated by insisting the gain is a small rational.
why_it_generalizes: Disentangling confounded mechanisms by imposing discreteness priors on one of them is a broadly useful identification strategy in mechatronics and metrology.
seed_example: gen.py emits (input_shaft_angle[t], output_angle[t]) traces from a two-stage gear train with a hidden rational ratio p/q (small integers) and a backlash deadband of hidden width that engages only on direction reversal, trained on long unidirectional sweeps with one or two reversals; the solver prints ratio, deadband width, and the hybrid update law. verify.py scores output-angle error on held-out rapidly oscillating drives (many reversals) plus parsimony. Trap: least-squares gives an irrational gain absorbing the backlash as bias, fine on sweeps, but on oscillating inputs the accumulated reversal losses dominate and only an explicit deadband state tracks them.

innovation_hook: Adaptation and carryover pull in opposite directions, so the right order is a band-limited zigzag that keeps the perceptual gain centered rather than any monotone sweep.
why_it_generalizes: Measurement processes whose sensitivity is set by their own recent history (sensors, ADCs, human raters) all need this anti-monotone sequencing logic.
seed_example: gen.py emits samples with true intensity vectors; the perceived value of the t-th sample is distorted by an adaptation state (a running EMA that compresses gain) and by contrast with the immediately previous sample, and a small budget of palate-cleanser insertions resets adaptation. Output: an ordering with cleanser placements; verify.py computes negative total perception error deterministically. Trap: monotone light-to-dark ordering, the sommelier recipe, minimizes carryover but drives adaptation into saturation; optimal orders zigzag within intensity bands and spend cleansers only at planted high-value comparisons.

innovation_hook: An upgrader's value is summed over all flow through it, so the problem is facility location embedded inside routing; the winning move is designing flows to CREATE high-traffic hubs worth upgrading.
why_it_generalizes: Jointly placing restorative infrastructure and routing degradable flow covers CDN transcoding, signal repeaters, and logistics cold chains.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a DAG (V<=5000) with per-edge multiplicative quality-decay factors, sources with initial quality, and sinks paying by delivered quality above thresholds; solver places K upgrader units on nodes (each restores quality at an energy price) and routes integral flow. chk.cc propagates quality along chosen paths and scores revenue minus energy. Trap: least-decay path routing ignores that one shared upgrader turns many long cheap paths into the best ones — placement and routing are inseparable, and hub consolidation beats per-path optima.

innovation_hook: The melody is a walk in a product automaton of its own shifted copies — building the self-compatibility graph explicitly and hunting long high-entropy cycles replaces impossible lookahead with graph search.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches converting self-referential constraints into an explicit product-space structure, useful wherever an object must be compatible with transforms of itself.
seed_example: Input: a consonance table over intervals mod 12, a voice list of (delay d_i, transposition t_i) pairs, and a length cap. Solver outputs an integer melody (pitch, duration per note); checker overlays every transformed copy and verifies all simultaneous pitch pairs are consonant, then scores melody length x pitch-class entropy + distinct contour-trigram count. Trap: drones and simple arpeggios pass consonance trivially with near-zero entropy, while greedy note-by-note extension deadlocks because each note must be consonant with several shifted copies of notes not yet written.

innovation_hook: Displacement is governed by the compliance matrix in which a few load-path members dominate — invert that sensitivity structure instead of sizing bars locally.
why_it_generalizes: Targeting a global response through the dominant terms of an inverse operator generalizes to structural, thermal, and circuit design.
seed_example: Input: a fixed truss geometry (nodes, bars, supports) under fixed external loads, a target displacement profile at the loaded nodes, and a discrete cross-section catalog. Solver outputs a cross-section index per bar. Checker assembles the stiffness matrix, solves K·u = F, and scores ||u−u_target|| + cost·(total mass). Trap: fully-stressed design (size each bar to its own stress limit) neither hits the target displacement nor minimizes mass, because displacement is a globally coupled response.

innovation_hook: A conserved quantity is the null vector of the transition's variation, not a fit of the forward map; only the invariant extrapolates.
why_it_generalizes: Distinguishing 'fit the trajectory' from 'find the invariant' is the essence of physical discovery.
seed_example: Input = many sampled (state -> next_state) transitions of a deterministic map, but only from a restricted region of state space (train regime). The solver outputs a symbolic expression I(state) from a declared operator grammar claimed invariant; verify.py evaluates it on a held-out extrapolation regime and scores the fraction of transitions with |I(next)-I(state)| below tolerance, minus a complexity penalty. Trap: regressing next_state from state fits the dynamics locally but fails to extrapolate; finding a combination in the null space of the state-variation (a true conserved quantity) generalizes to the unseen regime.

innovation_hook: Track the shadow value of remaining inventory against remaining time and price to the demand the future can still absorb, not to today's willingness-to-pay.
why_it_generalizes: Trains revenue management under elastic demand and a hard perishability deadline, transferring to pricing, yield management, and any depletable-with-expiry resource.
seed_example: A seeded demand model where the price you post deterministically shifts the arrival rate; inventory is fixed and perishes worthless at the horizon. Each step the program sets a price and realized sales deplete inventory. The evaluator sums revenue across seeds. The trap: myopic revenue-per-sale pricing either sells out early too cheaply or holds prices too high and lets stock perish unsold.

innovation_hook: Barriers are strongest against the flow's weak axis — steering beats stopping, so optimal designs are diagonal and 'open', contradicting the protective-wall instinct.
why_it_generalizes: Trains decomposing an adversarial quantity (momentum) into components and attacking the cheap one, a leverage-finding move useful across defense/containment problems.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits slope topography, release zones with mass, village cells, and a barrier-length budget; the contestant prints barrier segments with integer heights. chk.cc runs a deterministic granular routing sim (mass moves downslope with momentum, barriers deflect until overtopped) and scores mass entering village cells (minimize). Trap: one big arresting dam directly uphill of the village is overtopped by the flow's full momentum; chains of small oblique deflectors that split the flow and lengthen its path dissipate energy geometrically more cheaply, though the layout looks like it leaves the village exposed.

innovation_hook: You must run the automaton's logic backward or exploit its invariants to find a precursor, rather than seeding the target you want to see.
why_it_generalizes: Choosing initial conditions of a deterministic dynamical system to hit a late-time goal is inverse-design common across simulation-driven engineering.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits an NxN torus, a specified deterministic totalistic CA rule, a step count T, a seed-cell budget B, and a target pattern. The solver outputs up to B initially-live cells. chk.cc evolves T steps and scores cell-wise match to the target at step T. Trap: seeding the target shape directly fails because the rule scrambles it within a few steps; the win is to invert the dynamics (or exploit conserved activity/period structure of the rule) to find a small precursor that deterministically GROWS into the target at exactly step T.

innovation_hook: The planted mechanism is a second-order term that is statistically invisible in the training regime but asymptotically dominant, punishing curve-fitting and rewarding hypothesis-driven decomposition.
why_it_generalizes: Recovering interaction terms that only manifest at scale is the core skill of scaling-law science, variance analysis, and reliability engineering.
seed_example: gen.py simulates assemblies of n components whose deviations are sigma_i-scaled with a hidden pairwise correlation rho between same-batch neighbors, yielding total deviation D(n, sigmas, batch layout) = sqrt(sum sigma_i^2 + 2 rho sum_adjacent sigma_i sigma_j); training data covers n in 2..6 with varied sigmas and batch layouts, the held-out split has n in 12..24. Submission is a program computing D from the spec; verify.py scores extrapolation error on the held-out split only. Trap: a black-box regression or pure-RSS law fits n<=6 almost perfectly (the correlation term is small there) and collapses at n=24 where adjacent-batch correlation dominates; recovering the structural cross-term is the only path to extrapolation.

innovation_hook: The targets' pairwise ratios generate H, so computing H first and stocking coset representatives plus a short generator chain for H makes every non-decoy target a 2-3 factor product — the table should encode the subgroup, not the group.
why_it_generalizes: Preprocessing/query trade-offs optimized by inferring the invariant subgroup of the query distribution transfer to caching and codebook design broadly.

Trap: a baby-step-giant-step style table sized for the whole group costs ~sqrt(|G|) per target and is dominated by decoy handling.

innovation_hook: Only the true rational function, not a per-length fit, survives the longer held-out regime.
why_it_generalizes: Extracting a generative mechanism instead of a lookup is the essence of scientific generalization.

Trap: memorizing the train table or fitting per-length rules matches train but collapses on longer inputs. innovation_hook: A worst-of scoring rule converts frequency mining into an equalization problem — dictionary space must be allocated by each domain's marginal worst-case gain, and cross-domain substrings are worth double their frequency suggests.
why_it_generalizes: Budgeted resource equalization across heterogeneous consumers under a min objective is ubiquitous, from multi-tenant capacity to multi-task model capacity.

Trap: filling the dictionary with globally most-frequent substrings overfits the largest domain and the worst-document score collapses; the ceiling finds cross-domain shared stems and gives each domain a marginal-gain-balanced quota, equalizing the minimum.

innovation_hook: SNR is unimodal in launch power (linear ASE gain versus cubic Kerr penalty), so there is an interior optimal power rather than 'more is better'.
why_it_generalizes: The signal-versus-nonlinear-noise unimodality recurs in amplification, power control, and any gain-with-nonlinear-cost system.
seed_example: Input: an optical link topology with span lengths, discrete amplifier placements, and launch-power levels. Solver chooses amplifier spacing and per-span power; verify.py accumulates linear amplifier (ASE) noise plus Kerr nonlinear noise proportional to power^3 per span, computes each node-pair SNR, and reports the count of pairs above the SNR threshold (maximize). Trap: cranking power to the max or spacing amplifiers evenly fails because the cubic nonlinear penalty overwhelms the linear signal gain past an optimal launch power.

innovation_hook: You cannot outrun a comb by shifting the spectrum — you must tear holes in it; recognizing that periodic modulation creates band gaps converts a hopeless chase into a direct construction.
why_it_generalizes: The shift-versus-gap distinction (move the system vs restructure its spectrum) applies to interference avoidance in scheduling, communications, and materials.
seed_example: Input: a discretized beam of S segments, a stiffness budget to allocate across segments (integer units), and a comb of forcing frequencies with weights. Output: per-segment stiffness. verify.py computes all eigenfrequencies deterministically and scores the total resonant amplification sum_f w_f / (min_j |omega_f - omega_j| + eps). Trap: uniform stiffening rescales the whole spectrum, keeping the same collisions with the evenly spaced forcing comb (the instances plant the comb at the uniform-beam spacing); the escape is periodic stiffness modulation that opens band gaps exactly at comb frequencies rather than shifting modes wholesale.

innovation_hook: Under depensation, pulse fishing -- alternating closures with engineered bursts -- strictly dominates every constant quota, a structure readable in the printed recurrence but invisible to steady-state reasoning.
why_it_generalizes: Scheduling extraction against nonlinear renewable dynamics plus a racing agent population teaches respect for regime structure and hard survival constraints, transferable to caches, battery cycling, and epidemic budgets.

Trap: any constant MSY-style quota derived from steady-state analysis drags the stock through the depensation dip during the printed bad regime and zeroes the run.

innovation_hook: A cost model that exponentially punishes depth makes the canonical optimal algorithm the worst viable answer, forcing genuine restructuring rather than recall.
why_it_generalizes: Re-deriving what is optimal under a shifted cost model (instead of pattern-matching to cached optima) is exactly the transfer skill RL should amplify.
seed_example: Input: a family of polynomials in expanded form; solver outputs an arithmetic circuit; counter.py verifies correctness on seeded points and scores mult_count * 2^(mult_depth), an FHE-style leveled cost. Planted: the polynomials are symmetric-function compositions admitting log-depth balanced evaluation with modest count. Trap: Horner's rule — the universally correct answer for minimizing multiplications — has linear depth and thus an exponentially bad score; the solver must recognize that the objective inverts the usual optimality.

why_it_generalizes: Scaling-collapse reasoning underlies neural scaling laws, finite-size analysis, and any extrapolation toward a singular point.

Trap: polynomial or exponential extrapolation in T is smooth through Tc and misses the non-analytic vanishing; plotting rescaled data to find the collapse that maps all (T,h) curves onto ONE curve is the step recipes skip.

innovation_hook: The machines interfere through a shared environmental state, so optimal parallelism is an interior optimum computable from the congestion law, not maximal fan-out.
why_it_generalizes: Shared-resource congestion feedback (thermal budgets, bandwidth, shared coolant) is everywhere; learning that parallelism has an interior optimum breaks a very common greedy recipe.
seed_example: gen.py emits P panels, each an ordered chain of coats with base drying times; the actual drying rate of every wet coat is divided by (1 + c*number_currently_wet), and a single worker applies coats instantaneously at chosen times. Output: a timed application schedule; verify.py simulates the humidity-coupled drying and reports makespan. Trap: starting every panel immediately is the obvious throughput move, but the congestion term makes drying effectively quadratic in parallelism; staggered cohorts whose size depends on c and chain position dominate, and the best stagger varies along the chains.

innovation_hook: Against a white-box manipulation generator, the design goal is not fairness in the abstract but minimizing the sensitivity of the winner function to b-ballot perturbations of the specific sweep profiles.

innovation_hook: Minimizing gates hinges on committing to shared partial sums that many outputs will reuse, which no per-output greedy pass finds.

Trap: computing each output row independently is quadratic in weight; the optimum discovers common subexpressions (partial XOR sums shared across rows) and commits to building the intermediates that serve the most future outputs — a hard combinatorial search where early wire choices constrain all later reuse.

innovation_hook: Two orthogonal error processes with separate reset currencies must be co-placed: the value of a reset depends on where the other reset type already broke the sequence.

Trap: ascending concentration sort kills carryover but lets drift accrue and violates the position windows; the two reset types interact, so optimal plans sort within drift epochs and place both resets jointly where the sorted structure is forced to break.

seed_example: evaluator.py runs the submitted ordering-policy program (observes day, on-hand stock, trailing demand; returns order quantity with 3-day lead time) over 30 fixed demand timelines containing embargo windows (zero supply) and demand spikes, with holding and stockout costs, and scores the minimum profit across timelines. Trap: base-stock policies tuned to mean demand go bankrupt inside embargoes, while permanent hoarding loses to holding costs on the calm timelines; the sweep's embargoes are preceded by identifiable ramp precursors, so the ceiling is a policy that detects precursors and pre-hoards only then.

innovation_hook: The problem couples spectral leverage estimation with optimal-stopping discipline — the score punishes exactly the myopic marginal-gain reflex that offline spectral heuristics teach.
why_it_generalizes: Budgeted irrevocable decisions against a nonstationary stream, with a global objective only measurable at the end, is the shape of most real sequential resource allocation.
seed_example: Submission: a Python program that, for each arriving candidate edge (with cost), irrevocably accepts or rejects it under a total budget, aiming to maximize the final graph's lambda_2. evaluator.py replays a frozen set of seeded streams where high-leverage bottleneck-bridging edges are planted to arrive disproportionately late, and scores mean final lambda_2. Trap: greedy accept-by-immediate-lambda_2-gain exhausts the budget on early mediocre edges; strong policies maintain a cheap Fiedler-vector sketch, estimate stream statistics from the prefix, and pace acceptance thresholds so budget survives to the late high-value phase.

innovation_hook: Minimal-move and minimal-cost solutions diverge when the metric is nonuniform; the plant rewards decomposing the permutation into cost-aware primitive families.

innovation_hook: The objective is controlled by hole geometry, an emergent state of the allocator, so minimizing the obvious surrogate (live bytes) is provably not minimizing the score.

innovation_hook: The score turns safety reasoning into money: you profit exactly as much as you can PROVE non-interference (a dependence-graph coloring of moves into few simultaneous blocks), which rewards building an analysis, not a path planner.

Trap: barrier-after-every-step is trivially safe but pays ~T*B; the planted instance has large independent clusters, so near-ceiling programs prove independence and use O(log) barriers.

innovation_hook: The activation sets are not arbitrary — they are one geometric family — so the winning assignment encodes a spatial coordinate into channels rather than solving 40 coloring problems.

Trap: optimally coloring the base graph ignores ducting and one activation set creates mass conflicts among reused channels; since activation sets share a directional structure, a channel gradient aligned with that axis resolves most activated edges in every scenario at once.

innovation_hook: Two confounded mechanisms (process and measurement) become separable precisely because the announced intervention acts on only one of them — use the regime shift as the identification instrument.
why_it_generalizes: Factoring process from observation and exploiting interventions for identifiability is the backbone of causal inference and real-world sensor modeling.
seed_example: gen.py emits daily REPORTED case counts that equal true SIR-like dynamics passed through a hidden reporting law (day-of-week multipliers plus a soft testing-capacity saturation), during a pre-intervention growth phase; the solver prints both the dynamic parameters and the reporting operator. verify.py scores reported-count predictions in a held-out post-intervention regime where transmission drops by a known factor, plus parsimony. Trap: fitting reported counts as if they were the epidemic state absorbs the weekly ripple and saturation into wrong dynamics that fail the moment the regime changes; the intervention only transforms the LATENT dynamics, so a factored model extrapolates while a conflated one cannot.

innovation_hook: Curb slots, not vehicles, are the contended resource — policies that schedule CURB RESERVATIONS first and fit vehicle routes to them invert the standard dispatch decomposition and dominate.

Score: sum of rider wait + detour + circling penalties, deterministic. Trap: nearest-vehicle instant dispatch sends multiple vehicles to the same hot curb and forgoes pooling; strong policies hold requests briefly to form pools AND spread pickups across adjacent curbs, trading a known wait for contention avoided.

innovation_hook: Optimal sample placement is a 1D k-median under the query measure with asymmetric (one-sided) distance — recognizing the one-sidedness (samples only help from below) breaks the symmetric-clustering habit.
why_it_generalizes: Budgeted precomputation placed against an observed query measure is the design pattern of samples, sparse indexes, and lookup-table tiers everywhere.
seed_example: Input (testlib): a bitvector of 1e7 bits and a fixed trace of 1e6 rank queries whose positions cluster in drifting hotspots, plus a budget of B sample slots (each stores a precomputed rank). Solver prints the B sampled positions; checker answers each query by scanning words from the nearest sample at or below the position, charging one probe per 64-bit word scanned. Trap: uniform sampling every n/B bits is the universal textbook layout, but the query distribution concentrates 90% of probes in 5% of the vector — yet naive proportional density oversamples plateaus where the scan is short anyway, since cost depends on the query-to-sample gap distribution, not the density alone.

innovation_hook: Two independence constraints together are exactly matroid intersection — solvable optimally by augmentation, invisible to one-constraint greedy.
why_it_generalizes: Spotting matroid structure converts a messy dual-constraint selection into a solved problem class.
seed_example: Input = a ground set of weighted items with two simultaneous structural constraints (e.g., a partition/color budget AND an acyclicity/graphic constraint), up to a few thousand items. The solver outputs a chosen subset; the C++ checker validates both independence conditions and scores total weight. Trap: greedy-by-weight satisfies one constraint but violates the other and lands far from optimal under both; recognizing the two constraints as matroids lets an augmenting-path (matroid intersection) search find the true max-weight common independent set.

innovation_hook: Round along the fractional cycles two variables at a time (pipage), preserving marginals and feasibility, then repair residual violations with value-improving swaps.

The evaluator computes assigned value against the LP bound. Trap instances construct fractional cycles where independent rounding violates capacities or discards value, so rounding each variable to its nearest integer fails.

innovation_hook: Spread thermal load spatially and preemptively idle nodes before the hysteresis trap, since escaping a throttle costs far more than avoiding it.
why_it_generalizes: Trains placement under spatial accumulation and hysteretic penalties, transferring to thermal, congestion, and any control where crossing a threshold locks in a costly regime.
seed_example: A seeded workload stream is placed on a rack grid where heat accumulates at each node and diffuses to neighbors; crossing a temperature limit triggers a throttle with hysteresis that persists until the node is well-cooled. Each step the program places or migrates jobs. The evaluator sums completed compute across seeds. The trap: packing jobs onto the coolest node now builds a hotspot that throttles a whole neighborhood for many steps.

innovation_hook: Delivering the most packets means reserving contended time-slots for the future packets that will have no detour, not shortest-pathing each in isolation.
why_it_generalizes: Committing shared time-slotted capacity under future contention is the structure of scheduling, network flow, and multi-agent path finding.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a graph, unit edge capacities per time-slot, and a set of packets each with a source, sink, and release time. The solver outputs a routed path (with timing) for each packet; once a packet uses an edge in a time-slot, that slot is permanently consumed. chk.cc verifies no slot is double-used and reports the number of packets delivered within a deadline. Trap: routing each packet along its shortest path greedily consumes shared bottleneck slots that later-released packets require, blocking them; the optimum reserves contended edge-time slots for packets that have no alternative, anticipating future demand on shared links.

innovation_hook: Global lines couple all regions, so the solution is a 1D x 1D spacing design that sacrifices defect-dense bands deliberately — a quarantine strategy no local packer discovers.
why_it_generalizes: Optimizing global grid lines against a spatial risk map with size-value curvature models wafer economics, image tiling for codecs, and agricultural plot layout.
seed_example: Input: an N x N wafer map with defect cells (clustered, seeded), and a die catalog: sizes (a x b) with values v(a,b) superadditive in area. Solver outputs full horizontal and vertical cut lines (each spans the wafer) plus an assignment of resulting rectangles to catalog dies (a rectangle yields a die only if the die fits and its cells are defect-free). The checker recomputes the grid partition and sums realized value. Trap: uniform pitch tuned to the single best die maximizes count but every defect kills a big expensive die; because lines are global, local defect avoidance in one region forces bad pitch elsewhere — the insight is aligning line spacings so defect clusters are quarantined into cheap-small-die bands while clean regions get big-die pitch.

innovation_hook: Myopic least-latency routing induces oscillation and congestion collapse; a policy that internalizes its own marginal load damps the feedback.
why_it_generalizes: Feedback-induced instability from greedy local decisions is a general control lesson for adaptive routing, load balancing, and scheduling.
seed_example: The submitted PROGRAM is a routing policy: given current per-edge loads and a packet's destination it outputs a next hop. The frozen evaluator streams a fixed distribution of traffic sequences through a network with superlinear edge latency, applies the policy online, and scores total delivery delay (minimize). Trap: always picking the currently-least-latency route (myopic selfish routing) makes flows pile onto whatever edge just looked good, oscillate, and trigger congestion collapse; a policy that anticipates its own added load stays stable.

innovation_hook: The facility-location instinct is right but the metric is wrong — recognizing that random-walk access is resistance geometry, and cheaply sketching that embedding, is the entire game.
why_it_generalizes: Choosing the metric that actually matches the process (walk, load, diffusion) before optimizing is a transferable diagnostic habit that pure algorithm recall never triggers.
seed_example: Input: a graph (n<=5000) built from dense blobs joined by thin paths, and a depot count k. Output: k depot vertices. Checker computes for every node the commute time to its nearest depot exactly (via Laplacian pseudoinverse diagonal tricks) and scores the average. Trap: k-median under hop distance places depots evenly along the thin paths, but resistance distance collapses inside dense blobs (parallel paths) and explodes along tendrils, so the true optimum crowds depots onto tendril ends the hop metric considers 'covered'; the planted instances maximize this hop-vs-resistance disagreement.

innovation_hook: Facets are a Wulff-shape consequence of anisotropic attachment, unreachable by any isotropic rule no matter how it is tuned.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches that qualitative shape classes come from the symmetry of the local rule, connecting micro-rule to macro-form.

Solver outputs an orientation-dependent attachment-probability field (anisotropy function) used by deterministic seeded-order Eden growth from a seed; chk.cc grows to target mass and reports shape match as Ratio. Trap: isotropic attachment gives a rounded Eden blob (KPZ universality) regardless of size, and producing sharp facets requires an attachment anisotropy matching the target's Wulff construction.

innovation_hook: When data look linear in the canonical coordinates, the residual curvature pattern is a hypothesis test between one mechanism and two competing ones — take the residuals seriously.
why_it_generalizes: Detecting hidden mixture-of-mechanisms behind a clean-looking trend is ubiquitous in kinetics, failure analysis, and performance modeling.
seed_example: gen.py provides noisy rate measurements k(T) over a mid-range temperature window, where the hidden law is the harmonic combination of two Arrhenius channels (one accelerating, one deactivating), so training data look like a single gentle curve; the solver prints the symbolic law. verify.py scores log-rate error at held-out temperature extremes where the two channels separate and the rate turns over non-monotonically, plus parsimony. Trap: the classic move — fit a straight line in ln k vs 1/T — has excellent R^2 in-window and misses the turnover entirely; the tell is a systematic, tiny curvature in Arrhenius coordinates that recipe-followers discard as noise.

innovation_hook: The generalizing hypothesis class is recursions over a latent state, not functions of time or features, and the extrapolation split is engineered so only the recursion survives.
why_it_generalizes: Inferring hidden state-update laws from behavioral logs is core scientific practice (system identification), and the extrapolation gate rewards mechanism over curve-fitting everywhere.
seed_example: gen.py generates logs of job sequences (load, material, idle gaps) with observed processing times produced by a hidden recursive wear law with partial recovery during idle; the public split has short sequences, and the held-out split scores prediction MSE on sequences 5x longer with unseen load mixes. Output: a predictive program or expression; verify.py evaluates only on the extrapolation split. Trap: flexible regressors and time-indexed curve fits nail the public split and collapse under extrapolation; only recovering the state-update recursion (wear as a latent accumulator with recovery) transfers to long horizons.

innovation_hook: The design object is the DISPATCH RULE itself — a few cheap structural probes (variance of nearest-neighbor distances, hull ratio) suffice to route each instance to the heuristic whose failure mode it avoids, plus shared preprocessing all branches reuse.
why_it_generalizes: Algorithm selection under worst-family scoring directly trains the scientist move of characterizing WHEN a method works instead of averaging over benchmarks.
seed_example: The submission is a program that reads a routing-like instance, may cheaply probe its features, and must produce a solution within a fixed step budget (counted, not timed); evaluator.py runs it over K=5 fixed instance families (clustered, uniform, corridor, hub-spoke, adversarial-grid) and scores the MINIMUM over families of mean(baseline_cost / achieved_cost). Trap: every pure textbook heuristic (nearest-neighbor, savings, sweep) is planted to fail badly on exactly one family, so the best single recipe plateaus far below a dispatcher.

innovation_hook: Tide windows quantize time into a small number of epochs, so the problem collapses to packing circles per-epoch with continuity constraints — an epoch-graph reformulation that lets you plan berth reuse like graph coloring instead of online packing.
why_it_generalizes: Quantizing continuous time via structural events into a small combinatorial core is a general modeling move (memory allocation epochs, spectrum auctions, room re-use scheduling).
seed_example: Input (testlib gen): a convex polygon anchorage, ships with (arrival, departure, swing radius, value), and the rule that a ship may enter or leave only during tide windows; while anchored its swing CIRCLE must stay in the polygon and never intersect another anchored ship's circle at any overlapping tick. Output: for each accepted ship, an anchor point and actual enter/leave ticks. chk.cc verifies geometry and windows exactly and scores total value anchored (Ratio vs reference). Trap: first-fit placement by arrival wastes the center on small early ships; big-radius high-value ships later find no hole — the packing must be planned across TIME, reusing spots vacated at tide boundaries.

innovation_hook: The classic per-trie DP silently assumes a separable budget; making the budget global turns the problem into a Lagrangian/market allocation across subtrees, which is the non-obvious reformulation.
why_it_generalizes: Recognizing and breaking a separability assumption in a known DP is a portable research skill applicable to many resource-coupled layout problems.
seed_example: Input (testlib): 2e5 binary strings (prefix-free rules, up to 32 bits) and a fixed lookup trace of 2e6 strings with skewed rule popularity, plus a total memory budget M. Solver prints a multibit-trie: each internal node declares a stride s (consuming 2^s space units); checker validates coverage, replays lookups charging one probe per node visited, and rejects layouts over budget. Trap: uniform-stride and per-subtree-optimal DP (Srinivasan-Varghese style) both fail because the tight global budget couples subtrees — a space unit spent widening a hot subtree's root is stolen from every other subtree, and the popularity skew makes the exchange rates wildly non-uniform.

innovation_hook: Without boosters the optimum is an electrical equilibrium (Thomson's principle) solvable as a linear system — recognizing the physics analogy replaces search, and boosters then perturb it into a discrete-continuous hybrid only the analogy-holder can navigate.
why_it_generalizes: Mapping an optimization to a known physical equilibrium and then handling discrete perturbations around it is a powerful, transferable reformulation skill.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a graph (V<=3000) with per-edge loss r_e*f^2, sources and sinks with required deliveries, and K discrete booster units adding fixed pressure gain where placed. Solver outputs edge flows plus booster placements; chk.cc verifies conservation and delivery and scores total dissipation. Trap: shortest-path or max-flow routing concentrates flow, which quadratic losses punish; planted near-parallel path bundles make current-division splitting win by a wide margin, and boosters shift the optimal split nontrivially.

innovation_hook: For monomial maps x^d the difference distribution is governed by the number of roots of (x+s)^d - x^d, computable in closed form via gcd(d-1, p-1)-type conditions — so scanning exponents d by an algebraic criterion (plus affine dressing) achieves near-planar flatness that no generic search approaches.
why_it_generalizes: Replacing a p^2-size empirical objective with a closed-form root-counting criterion demonstrates analysis-over-enumeration for uniformity design.
seed_example: gen.cpp gives an odd prime p (1000-8000); solver outputs a permutation pi of F_p; chk.cc computes for every nonzero shift s the multiset {pi(x+s)-pi(x)} and scores the total squared deviation of these difference-counts from perfectly flat (minimize). Trap: random permutations have chi-squared-scale deviation ~p per shift (total ~p^2), and local swap descent barely dents it because each swap touches all p shift rows.

innovation_hook: Assignment is an investment in machine state, so the policy should maintain a portfolio of specialized machine conditions rather than equalize load.
why_it_generalizes: State-shaping dispatch generalizes to fleet management, server thermal pools, and human task allocation, and directly punishes the ubiquitous load-balancing recipe.

Trap: shortest-queue or load-balancing dispatch ignores that each assignment edits future machine speeds; the strong policy shapes machine states deliberately, sacrificing one machine to abrasive work while curating another's polish for the valuable finishing jobs.

innovation_hook: Routes must be load-balanced against the scenario ensemble simultaneously in space (fire overlap) and time (congestion waves) — splitting neighboring rooms across different exits is counterintuitive under any single scenario but dominates the minimax.
why_it_generalizes: Static plans executed under contingent dynamics with congestion externalities generalize to traffic, datacenter failover, and disaster logistics.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a building graph (rooms, corridors with capacities, exits) and K=8 fire scenarios (node deletions + capacity reductions); the solver assigns each room one static exit route; chk.cc simulates deterministic time-expanded movement with corridor congestion under each scenario (blocked-route occupants use a fixed freeze-penalty rule) and scores the MAXIMUM clearance time. Trap: everyone-to-nearest-exit both concentrates congestion on the main stair and sends several rooms through the very corridors the fires occupy.

innovation_hook: The catalog hides autocatalytic subgraphs: a byproduct loop with gain g multiplies effective input by 1/(1-g), so synthesis becomes a hunt for positive-gain cycles under a footprint budget, not a pathfinding problem.
why_it_generalizes: Spotting feedback loops that turn linear pipelines into multipliers is a deep systems insight (compound interest, bootstrapping toolchains, metabolic pathways).
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a catalog of up to 400 module types (typed inputs -> typed outputs with fixed ratios, footprint, count limits), a raw input stream of type X, a target product Z, and a slot budget. Solver outputs a wired DAG of module instances; chk.cc propagates steady-state flows and scores Z output. Trap: shortest conversion path X->Z discards byproducts; instances plant 'loop-closer' modules that convert waste back into upstream feed, so top scores need branching chains with positive-gain byproduct cycles — pure path search plateaus around 60% of optimum.

innovation_hook: With intransitive outcomes, seeding must carve a beatable path through the whole bracket rather than merely spreading strong players apart.
why_it_generalizes: Committing an arrangement whose consequences propagate through deterministic knockout rounds models tournament design, scheduling, and matchup planning.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits N players and a deterministic pairwise outcome matrix that is intransitive (contains rock-paper-scissors cycles). The solver outputs a committed seeding of players into a single-elimination bracket. chk.cc plays out the bracket deterministically (winners advance) and scores whether/how far a designated target player advances. Trap: the usual strong-apart seeding assumes transitivity and can route the target into an opponent it deterministically loses to; because outcomes are cyclic, the win is to route the target's bracket path through a chain of specifically beatable opponents, anticipating who the target would meet at every round.

innovation_hook: With irrevocable cuts, empty area is not fungible — the policy must manage the shape grammar of remaining space and treat discards as options purchased against the known arrival mixture.
why_it_generalizes: Online resource commitment with irreversible fragmentation and a refusal option is the structure of memory allocation, ad slotting, and organ-matching queues.
seed_example: evaluator.py streams a seeded sequence of rectangles (dimensions and values drawn from a fixed mixture) to the submitted program one at a time; for each, the program must immediately either place-and-cut it on the sheet (requiring kerf clearance k around it, cuts are permanent so the freed region's shape is locked) or discard it for a small penalty; score = total placed value averaged over 50 fixed seeds. Trap: bottom-left-fill with never-discard is the obvious policy and plateaus hard — it fragments the sheet with kerf-locked slivers and burns space on low-value early arrivals; strong policies maintain skyline corridors sized to the known distribution and pay discard penalties strategically to keep contiguous space for the value tail.

innovation_hook: The 1% FP cap is not a constraint to satisfy but a currency to allocate across channels family-by-family — rebudgeting it non-uniformly dominates any global-threshold ROC reasoning.
why_it_generalizes: Treating an aggregate error budget as an allocatable resource across heterogeneous sub-detectors generalizes to alerting, testing, and statistical multiple-comparison design.
seed_example: evaluator.py fixes a benign event set (hard constraint: false-positive rate <=1%) and an attack suite of 6 families, each graded from overt to near-benign stealth levels; the submission is a JSON of per-channel thresholds plus fusion weights. Score = minimum over families of the detection margin at that family's stealthiest detected level, zero if the FP cap is violated. Trap: maximizing average detection pushes all thresholds to a common operating point where the stealthiest family slips exactly under; the FP budget is a divisible resource, and the ceiling spends unequal slices of it on the channels where each family's fingerprint is most separable.

innovation_hook: Optimal pricing sacrifices local margin in high-externality regions to ignite a network-wide adoption cascade, trading local profit for global volume.
why_it_generalizes: Pricing under positive network externalities governs platform, standards, and diffusion-driven markets.

Trap: charging every region its myopic profit-maximizing price suppresses the externality feedback; subsidizing high-externality 'influencer' regions below margin triggers a self-reinforcing adoption cascade that lifts revenue elsewhere.

innovation_hook: Public finance's bunching-estimator trick -- reading the mechanism out of a distributional atom rather than the regression curve -- converts a hopeless extrapolation into an exactly determined inversion.
why_it_generalizes: Inferring the rule agents optimize against from equilibrium footprints (not input-output fits) is a distinctive scientific skill; distributional signatures as identification generalize to pricing, quotas, and rating systems.
seed_example: gen.py emits 40k (wage, chosen hours) pairs from utility-maximizing workers under a hidden piecewise tax with one notch and two kinks, with training wages spanning only the middle regime. Trap: directly fitting hours(wage) nails the training regime and whiffs the held-out bracket entirely -- the excess mass bunched in the hours histogram is the only signal pinning the notch and the schedule above it.

innovation_hook: Choose the domain decomposition the law respects (prime factorization) instead of the one the data arrives in (integer order) — smoothness is a property of the coordinate system.
why_it_generalizes: Re-basing a problem onto its natural algebraic coordinates before fitting is a transferable act of representation discovery.
seed_example: gen.py provides noisy observations of f(n) for all n<=3000, where f is a hidden multiplicative arithmetic function determined by a simple law on prime powers (e.g., f(p^k)=p^k - a*p^(k-1) with small integer a per residue class of p mod 4); the solver prints the prime-power law. verify.py scores exactness of predicted f(n) on held-out n in [10^5, 10^6] (where new large primes appear), plus parsimony. Trap: any regression in n — smooth, spectral, or tree-based — matches n<=3000 and utterly fails at 10^5 because f is wildly non-smooth in n; factoring n and hypothesizing multiplicativity collapses the problem to a two-parameter law testable on small primes alone.

innovation_hook: Carryover makes batch cost directional, so the design object is a set of ascending chains interleaved to satisfy deadlines, not a clustering of similar jobs.
why_it_generalizes: Directional state carryover between batches (heat, pressure, pH) converts batching into chain-decomposition, a structure that appears across process industries.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits jobs (required soak temperature, size, due date) and furnace capacity V; the energy for a batch depends on the gap between its soak temperature and the decayed retained heat of the previous batch, so ascending temperature chains are nearly free while descending steps pay full price. The solver outputs batches and their sequence; chk.cc computes energy plus weighted tardiness. Trap: clustering jobs by similar temperature, the standard batching rule, ignores the carryover direction; but pure ascending order wrecks due dates, so strong schedules braid several short ascending chains against the deadline structure.

innovation_hook: Compression here is not about space but about buying cache residency for OTHER data — a cross-region externality that per-tile local reasoning cannot see.
why_it_generalizes: Treating compression/precision as a lever on effective memory hierarchy capacity transfers directly to ML inference and scientific computing layouts.
seed_example: Input (testlib): a 2048x2048 grid whose cells have known entropy classes (compressible regions given), and a fixed access trace of 3e6 (i,j) reads mixing row sweeps, diagonal walks, and hot patches. Solver prints a partition of the grid into rectangular tiles, each with a compression level from a fixed table (level determines tile byte size), plus a memory order of tiles; checker replays through an LRU of 2MB (byte-accurate), counting one fault per tile load, weighted by tile bytes. Trap: Morton/Hilbert order with uniform tiles is the reflex, but compression changes effective cache capacity per region — compressing cold-but-swept regions lets the hot patches fit resident, so tile shape, compression, and the trace's direction structure must be optimized jointly.

innovation_hook: Amplitude is a resonance overlap integral, so the real design variable is the structure's spectrum — mass placement is inverse eigenvalue design in disguise.
why_it_generalizes: Trains optimizing an object through its spectral fingerprint rather than its spatial appearance, a deep reformulation skill for dynamics, graphs, and signal problems.

Trap: adding ballast at the largest-swinging node lowers all modal frequencies together and can walk the second mode straight into the gust spectrum; the winning distributions split modes apart, sometimes removing the intuition-approved center ballast entirely.

innovation_hook: The object being optimized is the worst case of your own future certificate, turning measurement placement into a minimax game against the bound formula rather than a coverage problem.
why_it_generalizes: Budgeted adaptive sampling to tighten a sound worst-case certificate is the skeleton of verification-guided testing, safe Bayesian optimization, and sensor placement.
seed_example: evaluator.py holds seeded manufactured surfaces z(x,y) (smooth base + localized defects, known Lipschitz constant Lc published in the spec); the submitted program adaptively chooses up to n probe points (each returns exact z), then must output a certified bound D such that true max|z - nominal| <= D; score per instance = max(0, 1 - D/D_ref) if the bound is valid, 0 if any instance's bound is violated, averaged over the fixed instance set. Trap: uniform grid probing wastes budget on flat regions and forces a loose Lipschitz-padded bound; the insight is that the certificate itself (Lc times distance-to-nearest-probe) tells you where the bound is weakest, so probing should greedily attack the argmax of the current certified envelope — and defect priors from early probes should re-weight it.

innovation_hook: Querying at powers g^0, g^1, ... turns the sparse polynomial into an order-t linear recurrence (Ben-Or-Tiwari/Prony), and the planted AP exponent structure lets a further substitution x -> g^{x} collapse t unknown exponents into 2 parameters, halving queries below the generic sparse bound.
why_it_generalizes: Query design that converts unknown support into recurrence coefficients is the archetype of measurement-efficient system identification.

Trap: dense interpolation needs deg+1 >> Q queries and any evaluation-at-random-points strategy wastes the budget without the geometric-point trick.

innovation_hook: Edge costs are functions of the traversal schedule itself, so graph topology and visiting rhythm must be designed together, dissolving the static-MST abstraction.
why_it_generalizes: Time-coupled reference graphs govern clock sync, surveying, and sensor fusion; co-designing structure with schedule is a genuinely non-textbook composite skill.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits N array elements with positions and per-element drift rates; the session measures one pair per time step, and a measurement's error grows with each endpoint's drift since its own last involvement; final score is total alignment error propagated along each element's measurement path to the reference element. Trap: building a minimum spanning tree over static pair costs ignores that costs are created by the visit order; fast-drifting hubs must be re-touched rhythmically, so the answer is a time-braided star pattern no static tree resembles.

innovation_hook: Overtopping is a free detention reservoir, so levee design is choosing where to fail — score improves by deliberately weakening segments, which no monotone protect-more heuristic discovers.

innovation_hook: Recasting toolpathing as minimum-cost Eulerization with a pierce-vs-airtime exchange rate reveals savings that contour-based reasoning structurally cannot find.
why_it_generalizes: Route-inspection with junction costs and once-only shared edges generalizes to snow plowing, PCB routing, and pen-plotter optimization.
seed_example: Input: a fixed nesting layout given as a planar cut graph (edges = required cut segments, some shared between adjacent parts and needed only once), pierce cost P per torch-down, and airtime = Euclidean length of torch-up moves. Solver outputs a torch program (sequence of pierce, cut-edge chains, retract); the checker verifies every required edge is cut exactly once and no part-interior is crossed while torching, and scores P*pierces + airtime. Trap: cutting contour-by-contour (one pierce per part) is the universal CAM baseline and is far from optimal — the insight is that shared edges and T-junctions make this an Euler-path augmentation problem on the cut graph, where adding a few clever airtime 'phantom edges' pairs up odd-degree vertices and collapses many pierces into long continuous strokes.

innovation_hook: Under capacity dynamics the objective is a min-cut equalization problem in disguise — the correct siting reasons about surviving cuts per scenario, which distance-based intuition never touches.

innovation_hook: With fixed hashes and a fixed trace, false positives are deterministic events you can enumerate and individually buy off — the analytic FP formula everyone optimizes is the trap.
why_it_generalizes: Replacing expected-case analysis with exact instance-level accounting when the instance is fully known is a repeatable source of wins across systems.
seed_example: Input: 8 storage tiers each holding a known key set, a global bit budget B, fixed seeded hash families, and a lookup trace where absent keys cluster in specific hash regions and most present keys live deep. Solver outputs per-tier filter sizes and hash counts k_i (and optionally which redundant keys to ALSO insert shallow); checker actually constructs the filters, replays the trace, and charges tier-probe costs 1,2,4,...,128 whenever a filter passes (true or false positive). Trap: allocating bits proportional to set size minimizes average FP rate but not trace cost — the deep tiers' huge probe costs and the skewed absent-key distribution mean starving shallow filters and overspending on tier 7 wins, and the exact (not expected) FP replay rewards even adversarial choice of k to dodge the specific colliding keys.

innovation_hook: Serial extraction is a common-pool problem in temperature space; the optimality condition is an equalized marginal conversion value along the chain, which solvers must derive, not receive.

innovation_hook: Fold order is gated by which creases stay accessible and by the fold-parity invariant, so late folds must be planned before early ones bury them.
why_it_generalizes: Order-dependent layering with a combinatorial feasibility invariant models sheet metal, packaging, and any stack-and-block process.

Trap: folding to match the outline greedily buries a crease that a later required fold needs; feasibility obeys a mountain/valley parity (Maekawa/Kawasaki) invariant, so the crease order must respect both layer-access and parity.

innovation_hook: The cubic nonlinearity between removed material and compliance means curvature shaping is an inverse-design problem where the greedy monotone intuition (more cuts = more bend there) hits the strength wall first.
why_it_generalizes: Inverse design through a nonlinear local response with a survivability floor is the pattern of metamaterial design, antenna shaping, and dose planning.

innovation_hook: By Lam-Leung, vanishing sums of n-th roots decompose into rotated full prime cycles, so the slack in any representation is a nonnegative combination of p-term relations — the search reduces to integer programming over the prime-relation lattice, not over angles.

innovation_hook: The right objective lives in the Laplacian eigenbasis, not on edges: an assignment is good iff its frequency vector avoids the slow eigenvector subspace, which often means deliberately mixing dissimilar frequencies locally.
why_it_generalizes: Assignment problems where quality is a global operator norm rather than a sum of local affinities break the matching/clustering reflex and reward basis-change reasoning.

Trap: greedily seating similar frequencies adjacently minimizes local mismatch but loads the bottleneck edges with accumulated flow; the insight is that stress is an electrical flow problem — the assignment should make omega nearly orthogonal to the slow Fiedler-like eigenvectors, i.e., balance the frequency 'charge' across the graph's spectral cuts.

innovation_hook: Because domains share morphology, spending dictionary slots on stems (which compose with every domain's affixes under the greedy parser) strictly dominates memorizing surface strings — a factorization the pooled frequency count never surfaces.
why_it_generalizes: Building one vocabulary/basis that serves heterogeneous distributions under a frozen consumer algorithm is the tokenizer/codebook problem in miniature.
seed_example: gen.py emits K=6 corpora (~200KB each) from different synthetic 'domains' built from shared hidden stems plus domain-specific affixes; the solver outputs a dictionary of D=512 substrings; verify.py compresses each corpus with a FIXED greedy longest-match parser and scores the MINIMUM byte-savings ratio over corpora. Trap: mining frequent substrings from the concatenation memorizes whole domain-specific words and starves the smallest domain.

innovation_hook: Replication changes the problem from partitioning to set-cover shaping — the biggest wins come from making covers cheap, not from putting friends together.
why_it_generalizes: Denormalization-vs-locality tradeoffs under a duplication budget recur in columnar stores, feature stores, and CDN object bundling.
seed_example: Input: a record schema of 64 fields with byte widths, 1e4 fixed queries each touching a field subset with a row-count weight, and a replication budget allowing up to r field copies. Solver outputs a partition of fields into segments plus the replicas; checker charges each query the summed page counts (ceil of segment byte width times rows over page size) of the minimal segment set covering its fields, choosing the cheapest cover deterministically. Trap: clustering by pairwise co-access affinity misses that replicating one narrow hot field into two segments collapses whole families of covers, and that a rarely-queried wide field must be quarantined even against its affinity; exact vertical partitioning is NP-hard so structure mining plus local search is required.

innovation_hook: The plant's true state is contaminant concentration, not inventory — sometimes producing at a loss buys future capacity, a claim no myopic margin calculation supports.
why_it_generalizes: Slow poison-state dynamics with cheap-now/expensive-later feedback (tech debt, soil salinity, model drift) all require the same shadow-price reformulation.
seed_example: Input: daily scrap offers (volume, contaminant ppm, price), limited virgin metal, a hard ppm cap on melt output, and a fixed fraction of each day's output returning as internal scrap next period, concentrating contaminants. Solver outputs a daily buy/blend/produce plan; verify.py tracks the exact contaminant mass balance and scores profit. Trap: maximizing daily recycled fraction poisons the internal loop weeks later; strong solutions treat contaminant concentration as a slow state with a shadow price and run planned deep-dilution purge cycles even at a daily loss.

innovation_hook: Design in the seam-interface language first (boundary types as a constraint graph), then enumerate block interiors within each interface class using dihedral canonical forms to certify distinctness cheaply.
why_it_generalizes: Trains interface-first decomposition with quotient-space deduplication — a modular-design skill far beyond quilting.
seed_example: Input: B x B blocks of b x b half-square triangles, c colors, per-color global count tolerance, a diagonal-continuity spec. Solver outputs the full quilt; checker verifies all B^2 blocks are pairwise inequivalent under the dihedral group, color counts are within tolerance, and scores block count + total length of diagonal seam lines continuing across block borders. Trap: generating distinct blocks independently almost surely breaks seam continuity, while enforcing continuity by copying neighbors makes blocks dihedrally equivalent — the constraints pull in opposite directions.

innovation_hook: A mirror's value is an integral over sun positions minus interference with neighbors, so the marginal mirror belongs at the shading frontier rather than the geometric sweet spot.
why_it_generalizes: Trains optimizing placements whose values are coupled through occlusion — a submodular-interference structure common to sensors, antennas, and coverage design.
seed_example: gen.py emits a ground grid, a tower position and height, a discrete sun path (list of directions across the day), and a mirror budget; the solver outputs mirror positions. verify.py traces integer rays for each sun step with exact shading and blocking tests between mirrors and scores total energy delivered over the day. Trap: hexagonal densest packing near the tower maximizes noon output but self-shades catastrophically at low sun angles; optimal density decays with radius and is azimuthally asymmetric, tracking where the sun path actually spends time.

innovation_hook: The real leverage is in the discrete recoloring degrees of freedom that reshape the transition graph itself, not in touring the graph better.
why_it_generalizes: Choosing item variants to make a sequence of asymmetric switch costs collapse is the structure of setup-dependent lot scheduling, codec switching, and context-switch minimization.
seed_example: Input: L layers, each a set of regions with a required color class (each class lists 2-3 acceptable concrete colors), and an asymmetric purge matrix P[c1][c2] (dark-to-light costs far more than light-to-dark). Solver outputs, per layer, a concrete color per region and a printing order of regions; verify.py charges P for every transition including the carryover from each layer's last color to the next layer's first, and scores total purge volume. Trap: per-layer greedy TSP over transitions looks optimal layer-by-layer but ignores carryover chaining and the recoloring freedom — the strong solution picks concrete colors globally so consecutive layers end and begin in the same color, effectively making the expensive dark-to-light purge happen once per print instead of once per layer.

innovation_hook: Balanced words with maximal factor complexity are the Sturmian/rich family, not random or periodic strings.
why_it_generalizes: Finding the object that jointly optimizes two opposed criteria is a broadly reusable insight.
seed_example: Input: alphabet size a, a length window K, a per-letter balance/frequency constraint, and target length L, seeded. The solver outputs a word; the checker, only if the balance constraint holds, counts distinct factors of each length l<=K and scores their sum. Trap: a random word maximizes factor counts but violates the balance constraint (score zero), while a periodic balanced word has tiny factor complexity. The insight is that Sturmian/rich words hit near-maximal factor complexity while staying perfectly balanced, occupying the exact sweet spot.

innovation_hook: Worst-case-over-loads rewards members lying in the intersection of many load paths — a shared-subspace insight that per-case optimization plus union never discovers within budget.
why_it_generalizes: Finding single components that simultaneously serve many stress scenarios is the physical version of finding shared structure across tasks.
seed_example: Input: a node grid with supports, a total member-length budget, and 16 published load cases (gravity, four wind directions, moving point loads). Solver outputs the bar set with cross-section choices; verify.py solves deterministic linear elastic equilibrium per case (fixed dense solver, fixed ordering) and scores the minimum over cases of (yield stress minus max member stress), zero if any case is unstable. Trap: optimizing the dominant gravity case yields a frame with no lateral stiffness that scores zero on the wind cases; the ceiling finds diagonals that serve as load paths for several sweep members simultaneously instead of adding per-case bracing.

innovation_hook: Majority-dynamics tipping is achieved by nucleating self-sustaining local-majority domains, favoring dense pockets over global hubs.
why_it_generalizes: Zealot-driven tipping models norm change, standard adoption, and controllability of consensus systems.
seed_example: Input: a social graph, an initial opinion per node, and a target opinion. Solver picks a minimum set of stubborn 'zealot' nodes fixed to the target; verify.py runs deterministic synchronous majority dynamics to a fixed point and passes only if the whole network converges to the target, scoring the number of zealots used (minimize). Trap: placing zealots on the highest-degree hubs is wasteful because a hub gets out-voted by its own many neighbors; tipping needs zealots that dominate small local majorities and nucleate self-sustaining domains.

innovation_hook: Switching can beat averaging: because the factors need not commute, each phase only has to kill the other phase's slow subspace, a genuinely dynamical design criterion with no static analogue.
why_it_generalizes: Designing alternating operators whose weaknesses are mutually orthogonal is the core of splitting methods, alternating projections, and curriculum scheduling.
seed_example: Input: a graph whose edge set must be partitioned into a 'day' set A and 'night' set B (each edge used in exactly one shift), with per-shift edge-count caps; step time tau fixed. Output: the partition. verify.py forms exp(-tau L_A) exp(-tau L_B) restricted to the disagreement subspace and scores its spectral norm (the Floquet contraction factor per period). Trap: splitting edges to make each shift a balanced 'half graph' optimizes the average Laplacian, but the product of two exponentials is governed by non-commuting geometry — two complementary spanning trees whose slow eigenvectors are mutually misaligned contract strictly faster than any near-equal static split.

innovation_hook: Subsidy only buys accuracy where budget constraints truncate disagreeing trades -- replaying the printed sequences makes the marginal value of beta exactly computable, turning allocation into resource optimization with planted non-concave pockets.
why_it_generalizes: Allocating an incentive budget across sub-markets by simulating frozen participant behavior is a template for grants, bounties, and liquidity mining; it rewards building an internal replay model of the evaluator.
seed_example: Input: 100 binary questions, each with a printed ordered sequence of traders (belief, budget) and the true outcome. Output: LMSR liquidity beta_q per question with sum <= B. verify.py replays each sequence in closed form (each trader moves price toward belief until budget or belief binds) and scores summed log-score accuracy of final prices minus lambda times realized subsidy payout. Trap: uniform liquidity idles subsidy on questions where the trader sequence already agrees, and 'more liquidity where more traders' fails because deep-pocketed agreeing crowds need none -- the planted high-disagreement questions carry all the marginal accuracy.

innovation_hook: The machine's limit cycle is a resource: schedule to intercept the oscillation instead of forcing setpoints, turning waiting time into free control.
why_it_generalizes: Riding rather than fighting a system's natural dynamics generalizes to HVAC, duty-cycled hardware, and any bang-bang-controlled shared machine.
seed_example: gen.py emits a drink queue where each drink needs brew (~93C) or steam (~130C) temperature, with quality decaying in |temp error| and waiting time; the boiler follows a deterministic first-order thermal law with heater hysteresis, and flushes drop temperature instantly by a known law. Output: a timed action script (pull, steam, flush, idle); verify.py integrates the dynamics and sums drink quality. Trap: FIFO service or batching-by-type fights the thermal oscillation; the insight is temperature surfing, sequencing drinks to intercept the trajectory as it crosses each window and using flushes as free downward control.

innovation_hook: The unit of decision is the RENDEZVOUS (a scope-pair committing to a target window), so reformulating the input as a time-expanded pair-assignment graph — then sequencing slews within each scope — dominates any per-scope value chasing.
why_it_generalizes: Elevating the decision variable from agent-action to agent-PAIR-commitment generalizes to interferometry scheduling, two-person code review routing, and sensor-fusion tasking.
seed_example: Input: T telescopes at fixed sky-coordinate parks with angular slew speed limits, and transient targets appearing at known (time, position, initial value) whose value decays geometrically per tick; a target scores only if observed by TWO telescopes from different sites within the same 5-tick window, and each observation occupies the scope for o ticks. Output: per telescope, a timed pointing schedule. Checker replays slews exactly and sums the value of validly paired observations. Trap: each scope greedily chasing its highest-value reachable target rarely aligns pairs; scoring requires treating targets as edges in a temporal matching between scope pairs.

innovation_hook: Because releases cannot be recalled and travel with lag, you must pre-empt future inflow spikes and deficits rather than react period by period.
why_it_generalizes: Irreversible flow control against a lagged deterministic forecast is the structure of inventory, buffering, and cascade control problems.
seed_example: Input: a chain of reservoirs with capacities, a deterministic inflow schedule per period, downstream travel lags, and demand nodes with flood (overflow) and drought (deficit) penalties. Each period the solver outputs releases per reservoir (releases are irreversible; water routes downstream with lag). The checker simulates all periods and reports total penalty. Trap: releasing only to meet the current period's demand overflows when a forecast inflow spike arrives with no headroom; the optimum pre-releases several periods ahead to make room and pre-fills before a drought, accounting for the routing lag so water arrives exactly when demanded.
